paragraphs.body: I used to write a lot when I was younger, a practice I had fallen out of until recently when I joined this class. I never had any trouble coming up with the idea for my next story, and I would often get distracted while writing just thinking about every cool thing I could add. I was so caught up in the ideas I wanted to get down on the paper that I didn’t concern myself much for the execution of said ideas. While I still find the ideas I was exploring at the very least fun, when I actually reread my old stories, I’m just bored the entire time. The characters were all entirely flat. They were hardly even one dimensional. There was no moment in any story that I could read a scene and forget that I was reading a piece of writing. There was no moment I could imagine my characters as actual people rather than a vehicle for the story to happen. For my second story “Problem Players,” I really wanted to focus on creating an actual person.

annotations.comment: That’s both the joy and drudgery of writing, right there.

paragraphs.body: Anne Lamott’s Bird by Bird chapter on character helped me a lot in both elaborating on my own characters and also helping me more consciously recognize decisions that other authors have made in fleshing out theirs. This chapter is essentially a collection of tips, all really helpful, but one of the most helpful to me in my understanding of building character was when she quotes another man’s advice saying, “’The evidence is in, and you are the verdict.’” This part, especially to me, is the most applicable to all other elements of writing.

annotations.comment: That’s a great quote to use.

paragraphs.body: I found in writing my second flash fiction that once my characters were properly fleshed out, conflict and plot would naturally arise. At the beginning of creating my story, which is about a dungeon master slowly turning a group of players against him with his childish and controlling energy, I was at a loss for how the story of the campaign would unfold. It was only when I began to form the personalities and motivations of the other characters that I started to find where they would naturally butt heads. I decided that two players, Wyatt and Julian, would slowly enter a romance throughout the game. Since I had already defined the narrator as a person who values his version of the story over anyone else’s, I could now create a moment in the story where he sidelines this romantic plot, leading to the group splintering and fracturing. I also had Kevin, who usually caved to the narrator’s whims. I realized there was also an obvious conflict here – what if Kevin was pushed too far? These two conflicts that naturally arose just by examining what motivated my character ended up being a perfect cause for the ending in which he is left angry and alone.

Now rereading the stories in order to write this craft paper, I found myself noticing much more intentionality behind an author establishing a character in the way they did. For example, in Selena Anderson’s “Godmother Tea”, when the godmother first asks Joy about her problems, Joy goes on a paragraph-long exposition dump of all of her recent problems, even describing one as “embarrassingly typical” of her, only to verbally reply to the godmother, “’Ain’t nothing going on but the weather.” These two paragraphs tell me almost everything I need to know about Joy. She is debilitatingly aware of her problems, but she refuses to seek help or take the steps she needs to heal from them. Anderson could have just flatly stated that Joy suffers from depression, but instead, she did so by describing her awareness of her issues and then contrasting it with an immediate denial. Now that Anderson has established Joy’s depression, it naturally leads to later conflict with Andre, her ex. She knows that it isn’t healthy to see him or sleep with him, yet she does so anyway, a disconnect between her mind and body that was established early on.

annotations.comment: Yes, it’s amazing how clearly you can see this happening when you know to look for it.

paragraphs.body: Similarly, in Jane Pek’s “The Nine-Tailed Fox Explains,” I learned very early on that the narrator, a nine-tailed fox spirit, has very little attachment to her partners. She sees them more as a necessary tool to prolong her life than an actual romantic connection. However, Pek ends the story in a surprising way. The narrator sees how in love with an old friend her current partner is, and she decides to disappear in order so that he can pursue her. As she leaves, she reflects on the relationship, saying that despite the time with this mortal being only “a single pleat in an ocean,” she will feel unmoored without the sound of his snores or heartbeat. The narrator defies her earlier detachedness, making her decision to leave her partner all the more heartbreaking. Even though Pek established early on that this character has no qualms about switching from partner to partner, this rare moment of sadness tells me so much more about her as a person. When I was writing my story, I had a very clear goal in mind after deciding the dungeon master would be terrible. Everyone would move away from him, leaving him all alone. I think it worked for the story, but after reading this, I wonder now if I could have thought of a different way to end it. Perhaps I could have written a section at the end showing a rare bit of self-reflection or remorse – or perhaps even earlier in the story. By writing “Problem Players,” I think I’ve built a foundational experience with two-dimensional characters of little complexity. I’m interested now in creating a story where a character changes only in a minor way but enough to make the story mean something more.

annotations.comment: Sure, there are several options for how it can end, which is fun for you as the writer.

paragraphs.body: Another very helpful part of Bird by Bird’s character chapter is the constant focus on a character’s thoughts. Throughout the chapter, Lamott is always posing questions to use to think about character like, “What little things would your character write in their journals: I ate this, I hate that, I did this, I took the dog for a long walk, I chatted with my neighbor,” or “How would your main characters describe their current circumstance to a close friend, before and then after a few drinks?” A lot of the focus of this chapter is to insert myself into the thought processes of my characters.

annotations.comment: How we uniquely think is perhaps the most distinctive thing about us, and also very hard to capture on paper.
annotations.context_left: t, I did this, I took the dog for a long walk, I chatted with my neighbor,” or “How would your main

paragraphs.body: George Saunders’ “The Semplica-Girl Diaries” was a helpful reference for me in how to write in the mind of a character. The entire story is written from the father’s point of view as he journals his daily life. In every entry, the father will interrupt his thought process, stopping to add his opinion or extra information he thought was relevant. One especially notable time this occurs is when the father describes his daughter’s friend’s mom arriving at the party. He says, “Leslie’s mother arrives (in BMW)…” In putting the extra information in parentheses, it is distanced from normal exposition and is placed more in the category of optional information. Now the inclusion of this fact serves as another example of how important status is to this man. He found it notable to specify that it was a BMW. He is focused on the brand of the car in a similar way to his focus on SG’s. They are both status symbols. I also used interruptions and asides in my own writing, both to show his disdain for his players and his lack of self-awareness.

annotations.comment: I saw this connection to your second story!
I love that style of writing. I use footnotes in my own.

paragraphs.body: Pek’s “The Nine-Tailed Fox Explains” also does this quite frequently, however, it will also occasionally leave out information. As the fox spirit narrates, no names are ever used. This easily let me see how detached the fox spirit is from the world, not even finding the names of the people in her life important enough to include. The complexity of her character isn’t found in just what I am given, it is found in what she does not give me. I tried this in my own story as well. I didn’t want to outright say that my narrator was a terrible guy, I wanted it to be gleaned from the text. While I think that I was overall a bit too blunt in my approach, I still appreciate the fact that I was able to create a believable character without directly stating what he was like.

annotations.comment: I didn’t think it was too blunt! But I did wonder, if he was so terrible, why it took them a while to get out.

paragraphs.body: Characters commenting in small ways is an easy way for a world to feel more actively lived in and can extend to dialogue too. A character monologuing on their own for 10 minutes can get boring. Throw in a comment or two, however, and suddenly it feels much more like an actual conversation. Character is what I really want to focus on more in my future writing. I feel like if I were to become more experienced at creating compelling and interesting characters, much of the story, dialogue, and conflict I need will naturally form.

annotations.comment: A great goal! And this is a lovely paper. You’re a good writer, Cameron. I hope you will keep at it! (late)
